name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلِكُ إِنِّىٓ أَرَىٰ سَبْعَ بَقَرَٰتٍ سِمَانٍ يَأْكُلُهُنَّ سَبْعٌ عِجَافٌ وَسَبْعَ سُنۢبُلَـٰتٍ خُضْرٍ وَأُخَرَ يَابِسَـٰتٍ ۖ يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْمَلَأُ أَفْتُونِى فِى رُءْيَـٰىَ إِن كُنتُمْ لِلرُّءْيَا تَعْبُرُونَ (٤٣)
```

```english
[One day] the king said, ‘I saw [in a dream] seven fat cows being devoured by seven lean ones, and seven green ears and [seven] others [that were] dry. O courtiers, give me your opinion about my dream, if you can interpret dreams.’ (43)
```

```arabic
قَالُوٓا۟ أَضْغَـٰثُ أَحْلَـٰمٍ ۖ وَمَا نَحْنُ بِتَأْوِيلِ ٱلْأَحْلَـٰمِ بِعَـٰلِمِينَ (٤٤)
```

```english
They said, ‘[These are] confused nightmares, and we do not know the interpretation of nightmares.’ (44)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِى نَجَا مِنْهُمَا وَٱدَّكَرَ بَعْدَ أُمَّةٍ أَنَا۠ أُنَبِّئُكُم بِتَأْوِيلِهِۦ فَأَرْسِلُونِ (٤٥)
```

```english
Said the one of the two who had been delivered, remembering [Joseph] after a long time: ‘I will inform you of its interpretation; so let me go [to meet Joseph in the prison].’ (45)
```

```arabic
يُوسُفُ أَيُّهَا ٱلصِّدِّيقُ أَفْتِنَا فِى سَبْعِ بَقَرَٰتٍ سِمَانٍ يَأْكُلُهُنَّ سَبْعٌ عِجَافٌ وَسَبْعِ سُنۢبُلَـٰتٍ خُضْرٍ وَأُخَرَ يَابِسَـٰتٍ لَّعَلِّىٓ أَرْجِعُ إِلَى ٱلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ (٤٦)
```

```english
‘Joseph,’ [he said], ‘O truthful one, give us your opinion concerning seven fat cows who are eaten by seven lean ones, and seven green ears and [seven] others dry, that I may return to the people so that they may know [the truth of the matter].’ (46)
```

```arabic
قَالَ تَزْرَعُونَ سَبْعَ سِنِينَ دَأَبًا فَمَا حَصَدتُّمْ فَذَرُوهُ فِى سُنۢبُلِهِۦٓ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ (٤٧)
```

```english
He said, ‘You will sow for seven consecutive years. Then leave in the ear whatever [grain] you harvest, except a little that you eat. (47)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ يَأْتِى مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ سَبْعٌ شِدَادٌ يَأْكُلْنَ مَا قَدَّمْتُمْ لَهُنَّ إِلَّا قَلِيلًا مِّمَّا تُحْصِنُونَ (٤٨)
```

```english
Then after that there will come seven hard years which will eat up whatever you have set aside for them—all except a little which you preserve [for seed]. (48)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ يَأْتِى مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ عَامٌ فِيهِ يُغَاثُ ٱلنَّاسُ وَفِيهِ يَعْصِرُونَ (٤٩)
```

```english
Then after that there will come a year wherein the people will be granted relief and provided with rains therein. (49)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱئْتُونِى بِهِۦ ۖ فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُ ٱلرَّسُولُ قَالَ ٱرْجِعْ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ فَسْـَٔلْهُ مَا بَالُ ٱلنِّسْوَةِ ٱلَّـٰتِى قَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى بِكَيْدِهِنَّ عَلِيمٌ (٥٠)
```

```english
The king said, ‘Bring him to me!’ When the messenger came to him, he said, ‘Go back to your master, and ask him about the affair of women who cut their hands. My Lord is indeed well aware of their stratagems.’ (50)
```

```arabic
قَالَ مَا خَطْبُكُنَّ إِذْ رَٰوَدتُّنَّ يُوسُفَ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ ۚ قُلْنَ حَـٰشَ لِلَّهِ مَا عَلِمْنَا عَلَيْهِ مِن سُوٓءٍ ۚ قَالَتِ ٱمْرَأَتُ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْـَٔـٰنَ حَصْحَصَ ٱلْحَقُّ أَنَا۠ رَٰوَدتُّهُۥ عَن نَّفْسِهِۦ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٥١)
```

```english
The king said, ‘What was your business, women, when you solicited Joseph?’ They said, ‘Heaven be praised! We know of no evil in him.’ The prince’s wife said, ‘Now the truth has come to light! It was I who solicited him, and he is indeed telling the truth.’ (51)
```

```arabic
ذَٰلِكَ لِيَعْلَمَ أَنِّى لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِٱلْغَيْبِ وَأَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى كَيْدَ ٱلْخَآئِنِينَ (٥٢)
```

```english
[Joseph said], [‘I initiated] this [inquiry], that he may know that I did not betray him in his absence, and that Allah does not further the schemes of the treacherous.’ (52)
```

```arabic
۞ وَمَآ أُبَرِّئُ نَفْسِىٓ ۚ إِنَّ ٱلنَّفْسَ لَأَمَّارَةٌۢ بِٱلسُّوٓءِ إِلَّا مَا رَحِمَ رَبِّىٓ ۚ إِنَّ رَبِّى غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٥٣)
```

```english
‘Yet I do not absolve my [own carnal] soul, for the [carnal] soul indeed prompts [men] to evil, except inasmuch as my Lord has mercy. Indeed my Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful.’ (53)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ ٱلْمَلِكُ ٱئْتُونِى بِهِۦٓ أَسْتَخْلِصْهُ لِنَفْسِى ۖ فَلَمَّا كَلَّمَهُۥ قَالَ إِنَّكَ ٱلْيَوْمَ لَدَيْنَا مَكِينٌ أَمِينٌ (٥٤)
```

```english
The king said, ‘Bring him to me, I will make him my favourite.’ Then, when he had spoken with him, he said, ‘Indeed today [onwards] you will be honoured and trustworthy with us.’ (54)
```

```arabic
قَالَ ٱجْعَلْنِى عَلَىٰ خَزَآئِنِ ٱلْأَرْضِ ۖ إِنِّى حَفِيظٌ عَلِيمٌ (٥٥)
```

```english
He said, ‘Put me in charge of the country’s granaries. I am indeed fastidious [and]well-informed.’ (55)
```

```arabic
وَكَذَٰلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ يَتَبَوَّأُ مِنْهَا حَيْثُ يَشَآءُ ۚ نُصِيبُ بِرَحْمَتِنَا مَن نَّشَآءُ ۖ وَلَا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٥٦)
```

```english
That is how We established Joseph in the land that he may settle in it wherever he wished. We confer Our mercy on whomever We wish, and We do not waste the reward of the virtuous. (56)
```

```arabic
وَلَأَجْرُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ يَتَّقُونَ (٥٧)
```

```english
And the reward of the Hereafter is surely better for those who have faith and are Godwary. (57)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses encompass the story of his (peace be upon him) release from prison and his attainment of the high office and might of Egypt, along with the causes leading to that. Within this is the King’s secondary investigation into the accusation against him and the manifestation of his complete innocence.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقالَ الْمَلِكُ إِنِّي أَرى سَبْعَ بَقَراتٍ سِمانٍ يَأْكُلُهُنَّ سَبْعٌ عِجافٌ** — *“The king said, ‘I saw [in a dream] seven fat cows being eaten by seven lean ones...’”* (12:43) until the end of the verse. This was a vision (رؤيا) of the King which he related to the assembly (الملأ), and the evidence for this is his saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الْمَلَأُ أَفْتُونِي فِي رُءْيايَ** — *“O council, give me your opinion regarding my dream...”* (12:43). His saying: **إِنِّي أَرى** — *“I see”* is a narration of a past state (حكاية حال ماضية), and it is possible that it was a recurring vision, just as a similar possibility exists in the previous sayings: **إِنِّي أَرانِي أَعْصِرُ خَمْراً** — *“I saw myself pressing wine”* and **إِنِّي أَرانِي أَحْمِلُ** — *“I saw myself carrying...”* etc.

The word **السمان** (fat) is the plural of **سمينة**, and **العجاف** (lean) is the plural of **عجفاء**, meaning emaciated. It is mentioned in *al-Majma‘*: The form *fa‘lā’* (فعلاء) is not pluralized as *fi‘āl* (فعال) except for *‘ajfā’* (عجفاء) becoming *‘ijāf* (عجاف). The analogical plural (القياس) would have been *‘ujf* (عُجْف) — with a *damma* on the *‘ayn* and *sukūn* on the *jīm* — similar to how *hamrā’* (red), *khadrā’* (green), and *baydā’* (white) become *humr*, *khudr*, and *bīd*. Others have said that this [irregularity] is a matter of linguistic assimilation (اتباع), while the analogical plural remains *‘ujf*.

The term **الإفتاء** (giving a formal opinion) is the verbal noun of *aftā* (أفتى), derived from *fatwā* (الفتوى) or *futyā* (الفتيا). It is stated in *al-Majma‘*: **الفتيا** (the formal legal opinion) is the answer regarding the ruling of a concept (حكم المعنى), whereas sometimes the answer is regarding the concept itself, in which case it is not a *futyā*.

His saying: **تَعْبُرُونَ** — *“you can interpret”* is from *‘abr* (العبر), which is the explanation of the interpretation of a vision (تأويل الرؤيا), and it is sometimes called *ta‘bīr* (تعبير). In any case, it is derived from crossing (عبور) a river or similar things, as if the interpreter crosses from the vision to the interpretation that lies beyond it—which is the reality of the matter (حقيقة الأمر) that was manifested to the dreamer in a specific, familiar form.

It is mentioned in *al-Kashshāf* regarding His saying: **سَبْعَ بَقَراتٍ سِمانٍ** — *“seven fat cows”*: "If you ask: Is there a difference between making 'fat' (سمان) an adjective for the specified noun (المميز), which is 'cows' (بقرات), rather than the number (المميز), which is 'seven'—as opposed to saying 'seven cows, fat ones' (سبع بقرات سماناً)? I say: When you make it an adjective for 'cows', you intend to specify the seven by a particular type of cows, namely the fat ones among them, not by their genus (جنس). Had you described the 'seven' with it, you would have intended to specify the seven by the genus of cows, not a specific type, and then returned to describe that which was specified by genus as being fat."

"If you ask: Why was it not said 'seven lean ones' (سبع عجاف) as a genitive construction (إضافة)? I say: The specification (التمييز) is established to clarify the genus, and 'lean' (عجاف) is an adjective (وصف) by which clarification cannot occur on its own. If you then ask: But one says 'three horsemen' (ثلاثة فرسان) and 'five companions' (خمسة أصحاب)? I say: 'Horseman' (الفارس), 'companion' (الصاحب), and 'rider' (الراكب) and their like are adjectives that have come to function as nouns, so they take their rulings, and that which is not permissible for others becomes permissible for them. Do you not see that you do not say: 'I have three large ones' (عندي ثلاثة ضخام) or 'four thick ones' (أربعة غلاظ)?" End quote.

He also said: "If you ask: Is there evidence in the verse that the dry ears of grain were seven, like the green ones? I say: The discourse is built upon its focus on this number regarding the fat and lean cows and the green ears of grain; thus, the meaning of the latter must encompass the seven, and His saying: **وَأُخَرَ يابِساتٍ** — *“and others [that were] dry”* (12:43) means 'and seven others'. If you ask: Is it permissible to coordinate (عطف) His saying **وَأُخَرَ يابِساتٍ** to **سُنْبُلاتٍ خُضْرٍ** — *“green ears”* so that it takes the genitive position? I say: This would lead to a contradiction (تدافع). This is because coordinating it to 'green ears' would require it to enter into their ruling, thus becoming a specification for the aforementioned 'seven', while the word 'others' (أخر) requires it to be something other than the seven. To illustrate: you say, 'I have seven men, standing and sitting' (عندي سبعة رجال قيام وقعود) in the genitive, and it is correct because you specified the seven as men described as standing and sitting, such that some are standing and some are sitting. But if you said, 'He has seven men, standing, and others, sitting' (عنده سبعة رجال قيام وآخرين قعود), it would be a contradiction and thus invalid." End quote. His words, while containing a subtle point, do not yield more than a strong probability (الظن) that the dry ears were seven like the others; as for the necessity of such an indication from the text itself, it is not there at all.

The meaning of the verse is: The King of Egypt said to his assembly: "I see in my sleep seven fat cows being eaten by seven emaciated cows, and I see seven green ears of grain and other dry ears. O assembly, explain to me what you have of the ruling of my vision, if you are able to interpret visions."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالُوا أَضْغاثُ أَحْلامٍ وَما نَحْنُ بِتَأْوِيلِ الْأَحْلامِ بِعالِمِينَ** — *“They said, ‘[These are] confused nightmares, and we do not know the interpretation of nightmares.’”* (12:44). **الأحلام** (nightmares/dreams) is the plural of *hulm* (حُلُم) — with two *dammas*, though the middle may be vowelless — and it refers to what a sleeper sees in his sleep. The origin of its meaning refers to what is conceptualized by a person from within his own soul without reaching it through the senses. From this is the naming of the intellect as *hilm* (حلم) because it is the rectitude of thought. From it also is *hulm* (الحلم) for the time of puberty, as the Exalted said: **وَإِذا بَلَغَ الْأَطْفالُ مِنْكُمُ الْحُلُمَ** — *“When the children among you reach puberty...”* (24:59), meaning the time of maturity, the maturity of the intellect. From it also is *hilm* (الحلم) — with a *kasra* on the *hā’* — meaning forbearance (الأناءة), the opposite of recklessness (الطيش), which is the self-control of the soul and nature against the eruption of anger and the lack of haste in punishment, for this only arises from the rectitude of thought. Al-Raghib mentioned that the root of the meaning is *hilm* (الحلم) with a *kasra*, but this is not without affectation (تكلف).

Al-Raghib said: **الضغث** (a bundle) is a handful of herbs, grass, or twigs, and its plural is **أضغاث**. The Exalted said: **وَخُذْ بِيَدِكَ ضِغْثاً** — *“Take a bundle in your hand...”* (38:44). Diverse dreams whose realities are not clear are likened to this: **قالُوا أَضْغاثُ أَحْلامٍ** — *“They said: bundles of mixed dreams.”* End quote.

The naming of a single vision as "confused nightmares" (أضغاث أحلام) is as if by way of claiming it consists of scattered, mixed images gathered from different dreams, each having its own separate interpretation. When they are gathered and mixed, it becomes difficult for the interpreter to grasp their interpretation. A person often moves in a single sleep from one vision to another, and from those to a third, and so on; when their parts are mixed, they become "confused nightmares," and grasping their reality becomes impossible. This intended meaning is indicated by the expression "confused nightmares" (أضغاث أحلام) using the indefinite form (تنكير) for both the first and second parts of the genitive construction, as is not hidden.

Furthermore, the verse—namely His saying: **وَقالَ الْمَلِكُ إِنِّي أَرى** — *“The king said, ‘I saw...’”*—is not explicit in it being a single vision. In the Torah, it is mentioned that he saw the fat and lean cows in one vision, and the green and dry ears in another vision.

His saying: **وَما نَحْنُ بِتَأْوِيلِ الْأَحْلامِ بِعالِمِينَ** — *“and we do not know the interpretation of nightmares.”* If the definite article (الألف واللام) is for specific reference (للعهد), then the meaning is: "We are not knowledgeable in the interpretation of these specific dreams which are confused nightmares." If it is not for specific reference, and the plural with the definite article denotes generality (العموم), then the meaning is: "We are not knowledgeable in the interpretation of all dreams; we only interpret those that are not confused nightmares." In any case, there is no contradiction between their deeming his vision to be confused nightmares and their denial of knowledge regarding the interpretation of [such] dreams. Had the word *ahlām* (الأحلام) referred only to true visions, each half of their statement would have rendered the other redundant.

The meaning of the verse is: They said—meaning the assembly said to the King: "What you have seen are confused nightmares and mixtures of different dreams, and we are not knowledgeable in the interpretation of this type of dream," or "We are not knowledgeable in the interpretation of all dreams; we only know the interpretation of righteous visions (الرؤى الصالحة)."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقالَ الَّذِي نَجا مِنْهُما وَادَّكَرَ بَعْدَ أُمَّةٍ أَنَا أُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِتَأْوِيلِهِ فَأَرْسِلُونِ** — *“The one of the two who had been delivered said, remembering after a long time, ‘I will inform you of its interpretation, so let me go.’”* (12:45). **الأمة** (a period/community) is a group that is intended for a purpose, and its usage is predominant regarding humans. What is meant here is a "group" of years, which is the period during which this speaker—the King's cupbearer—forgot to mention Yusuf to his master (ربه), even though Yusuf had asked him to do so, but Satan made him forget the mention of his master, so Yusuf remained in prison for several years.

The meaning is: The one who was delivered from prison—one of Yusuf's two companions therein—remembered, after a group of years, what Yusuf had asked of him in prison when he interpreted his dream: "I will inform you of the interpretation of what the King saw in his sleep, so send me to Yusuf in prison so that I may inform you of its interpretation."

The plural address in his saying: **أُنَبِّئُكُمْ** — *“I will inform you [plural]”* and his saying **فَأَرْسِلُونِ** — *“so send me [plural]”* is an inclusion of those present with the King, namely the assembly (الملأ) of the pillars of the state and the supports of the kingdom who manage the affairs of the people. The evidence for this is his subsequent saying: **لَعَلِّي أَرْجِعُ إِلَى النَّاسِ** — *“so that I may return to the people,”* as will follow.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يُوسُفُ أَيُّهَا الصِّدِّيقُ أَفْتِنا فِي سَبْعِ بَقَراتٍ سِمانٍ** — *“‘Yusuf, O truthful one, give us your opinion regarding seven fat cows...’”* (12:46) until the end of the verse. In the discourse, there is an omission and an implied meaning for the sake of conciseness (إيجاز). The implied meaning is: "So they sent him, and he came to Yusuf in the prison and said: 'O Yusuf, O truthful one, give us your opinion regarding the King's vision...'" and he mentioned the vision and mentioned that the people were waiting for its interpretation. This style is among the subtle rhetorical styles of the Noble Quran.

He called Yusuf **صديق** (truthful one) — *one who is intensely and habitually truthful* — because of the truthfulness he had seen in Yusuf's interpretation of his own dream and his companion's dream in prison, and other matters he witnessed of his actions and speech in prison. Allah, the Glorified, has confirmed his being a *siddīq* by narrating this without rejection.

He mentioned the content of the vision without explicitly stating it was a vision, saying: **أَفْتِنا فِي سَبْعِ بَقَراتٍ سِمانٍ يَأْكُلُهُنَّ سَبْعٌ عِجافٌ وَسَبْعِ سُنْبُلاتٍ خُضْرٍ وَأُخَرَ يابِساتٍ** — *“give us your opinion regarding seven fat cows being eaten by seven lean ones, and seven green ears and others [that were] dry”* (12:46). This is because his saying **أَفْتِنا** — *“give us your opinion”* is a request for the ruling (حكم) that his insight leads to, and because the established matter between him and Yusuf was the interpretation of visions; likewise, the conclusion of the speech indicates this and reveals it.

His saying: **لَعَلِّي أَرْجِعُ إِلَى النَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ** — *“so that I may return to the people, so that they may know.”* (12:46). The first "so that" (لعل) provides the reason for his saying "give us your opinion," and the second "so that" provides the reason for his saying "I may return." The meaning is: "Give us your opinion regarding the matter of this vision, for in your opinion is the hope that I may return with it to the people and inform them of it, and in my return to them is the hope that they may know it and thereby emerge from confusion and ignorance."

From here it becomes clear that his saying **أَرْجِعُ** — *“I may return”* carries the meaning of "I may return with that [interpretation]." For it is known that if he gave an opinion on it and the seeker of the opinion returned to the people, his return would be the return of one knowledgeable of its interpretation and expert in its ruling; thus, his return to them at that time is a return accompanied by the interpretation that was conveyed to him. Understand this.

In his saying first: **أَفْتِنا** — *“give us [plural] your opinion”* and second: **لَعَلِّي أَرْجِعُ إِلَى النَّاسِ** — *“so that I may return to the people,”* there is an indication that he was seeking the opinion as a messenger from the King and the assembly, and he was not asking for himself so that he might learn it and then inform them. Rather, it was so that he might carry it to them. Therefore, Yusuf did not restrict the address to him alone but generalized the address to him and others, saying: **تَزْرَعُونَ** — *“You [plural] shall sow...”* etc.

In his saying: **إِلَى النَّاسِ** — *“to the people,”* there is an intimation or indication that the people were waiting for their confusion to be lifted by its interpretation. This is only because the assembly (الملأ) were the guardians of the people's affairs, and their choice in the matter was the choice of the people; or that the people themselves were in this state due to their attachment to the King and their concern for his vision, because a vision usually looks toward the affairs of life that a person is concerned with, and kings are only concerned with the affairs of the kingdom and the matters of the subjects.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ تَزْرَعُونَ سَبْعَ سِنِينَ دَأَباً فَما حَصَدْتُمْ فَذَرُوهُ فِي سُنْبُلِهِ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً مِمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ** — *“He said, ‘You shall sow for seven years consecutively. Then leave what you harvest in its ear, except a little that you eat.’”* (12:47). Al-Raghib said: **الدأب** (consecutiveness/habit) is the continuation of travel; one *da’aba* (labored) in travel *da’ban*. The Exalted said: **وَسَخَّرَ لَكُمُ الشَّمْسَ وَالْقَمَرَ دائِبَيْنِ** — *“He disposed the sun and the moon for you, both constant [in their courses]...”* (14:33). *Al-da’b* is the continuous habit (العادة المستمرة) that always remains in its state. The Exalted said: **كَدَأْبِ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ** — *“Like the custom of Pharaoh’s clan...”* (8:52), meaning like their habit which they continuously follow. End quote. Accordingly, the meaning is: "You shall sow for seven years in a successive, continuous sowing." It is also said: it is from *da’ab* in the sense of fatigue (التعب), meaning: "You shall sow with diligence and effort." It is possible for it to be a circumstantial qualifier (حال), meaning: "You shall sow being constant and continuous," or "being diligent and hardworking in it."

They have mentioned that **تَزْرَعُونَ** — *“You shall sow”* is a report (خبر) carrying the meaning of a command (إنشاء). Often a command is presented in the form of a report to emphasize the necessity of compliance, as if it is a reality being reported, such as His saying, the Exalted: **تُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللهِ وَرَسُولِهِ وَتُجاهِدُونَ فِي سَبِيلِ اللهِ** — *“You shall believe in Allah and His Apostle and wage jihad in the way of Allah...”* (61:11). The evidence for this is His subsequent saying: **فَما حَصَدْتُمْ فَذَرُوهُ فِي سُنْبُلِهِ** — *“Then leave what you harvest in its ear.”* It is said: He only commanded placing it and leaving it in its ear because the ear of grain is not infested by weevils and does not perish even if it remains for a period of time, whereas if it is threshed and winnowed, destruction hastens toward it.

The meaning is: "Sow for seven successive years, and what you harvest, leave it in its ear so that it does not perish, and preserve it thus, except for a little, which is what you eat during these years."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ يَأْتِي مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ سَبْعٌ شِدادٌ يَأْكُلْنَ ما قَدَّمْتُمْ لَهُنَّ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً مِمَّا تُحْصِنُونَ** — *“Then after that there will come seven hard [years] which will eat up what you have set aside for them, except a little which you preserve [for seed].”* (12:48). **الشداد** (hard/severe) is the plural of *shadīd*, from *shiddah* (الشدة) meaning difficulty, because of the difficulty and distress (الحرج) upon the people in years of drought and famine. Or it is from *shadda ‘alayhi* (he charged at him) when he attacks; this is more suitable for what follows of their description by His saying: **يأْكُلْنَ ما قَدَّمْتُمْ لَهُنَّ** — *“they will eat up what you have set aside for them.”*

Accordingly, the discourse contains a subtle metaphor (تمثيل), as if these years are predatory beasts (سباع ضارية) that charge at the people to prey upon them and eat them, so they present to them the food they have stored with them, which they eat and then depart from them.

**الإحصان** (preservation) is to safeguard (الإحراز) and store away. The meaning is: "Then after that—meaning after the mentioned years of plenty—there will come seven hard years that will press hard upon you, eating what you have set aside for them, except for a little of what you safeguard and store."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ يَأْتِي مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ عامٌ فِيهِ يُغاثُ النَّاسُ وَفِيهِ يَعْصِرُونَ** — *“Then after that there will come a year in which the people will be helped and in which they will press [wine and oil].”* (12:49). It is said: *ghāthahu Allāh* (Allah helped him) and *aghāthahu*, meaning He aided him. *Yughīthu* (he helps)—with the *yā’* having either a *fatha* or a *damma*—means He aids him, and it is from *ghawth* (الغوث) meaning aid (النصرة). And *ghāthahum Allāh* (Allah sent them rain) *yaghīthuhum* is from *ghayth* (الغيث) which is rain (المطر). So His saying: **فِيهِ يُغاثُ النَّاسُ** — *“in which the people will be helped/given rain”*—if it is from *ghawth*, its meaning is: "They will be aided therein by Allah, the Glorified, through the removal of distress, the lifting of drought and famine, and the sending down of blessing and bounty." If it is from *ghayth*, its meaning is: "They will be given rain, and thus the drought will be lifted from among them."

This second meaning is more suitable in view of His subsequent saying: **وَفِيهِ يَعْصِرُونَ** — *“and in which they will press.”* No attention should be paid to the claim of one who asserts that the first meaning is what is immediately understood from the context of the verse, except according to the reading of *yu‘sarūn* (يُعْصَرُونَ) in the passive voice, meaning "they are rained upon."

As for the objection raised by some Orientalists against the second meaning—that it does not apply to the case of the verse, for the fertility of Egypt only occurs through the flooding of the Nile, not by rain, so rains have no effect there—it is refuted by the fact that the flood itself only occurs due to the rain that feeds it in its courses from the lands of Sudan.

Furthermore, it is permissible that **يُغاثُ** is derived from *ghayth* in the sense of vegetation (النبات). It is mentioned in *Lisān al-‘Arab*: *Al-ghayth* is the herbage (الكلاء) that grows from the water of the sky. End quote. This is more suitable than the two previous meanings in view of His saying: **وَفِيهِ يَعْصِرُونَ**.

His saying: **وَفِيهِ يَعْصِرُونَ** — *“and in which they will press”* is from *‘asr* (العصر), which is the extraction of what is in a thing of water or oil by pressure, such as extracting the juice of grapes and dates for syrup and other things, and extracting the oil of olives and sesame for food, lighting, and other purposes. It is also possible that *‘asr* refers to milking (الحلب), meaning they milk the udders of their livestock, as some have interpreted it.

The meaning is: "Then after that—meaning after the mentioned seven hard years—there will come a year in which their lands will sprout—or they will be given rain, or they will be aided—and in it they will make drinks and oils from fruits and vegetables, or they will milk the udders of their livestock." In this is a metaphor for the abundance of bounty upon them and upon their livestock and cattle.

Al-Baydawi said in his *Tafsir*: "This is glad tidings he gave them after he interpreted the fat cows and green ears as fertile years, and the lean and dry ones as years of drought, and the lean ones swallowing the fat ones as the eating of what was gathered in the fertile years during the years of drought. Perhaps he knew this through revelation (الوحي), or by the fact that the end of drought is followed by fertility, or because the divine custom (السنة الإلهية) is to expand for His servants after He has restricted them." End quote. Others have mentioned something similar to what he mentioned.

The author of *al-Manār* said in his *Tafsir* regarding the verse: "The meaning is that this year is one of great fertility and prosperity, in which the people will have all they desire of bounty and luxury. The informing of this is an addition to the interpretation of the vision, for it is possible that the first year after the years of hardship and drought would be less than that. Thus, this specification and detail was not known to Yusuf except through revelation from Allah, the Mighty and Majestic; it has no counterpart in the King's vision, nor is it a necessary consequence of its interpretation with this detail." End quote.

What I see is that they have followed the path of leniency and approximation in explaining the verses of the vision and its interpretation. This is because if we contemplate his (peace be upon him) words in the interpretation—namely his saying: **تَزْرَعُونَ سَبْعَ سِنِينَ دَأَباً فَما حَصَدْتُمْ فَذَرُوهُ فِي سُنْبُلِهِ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً مِمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ ثُمَّ يَأْتِي مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ سَبْعٌ شِدادٌ يَأْكُلْنَ ما قَدَّمْتُمْ لَهُنَّ إِلَّا قَلِيلاً مِمَّا تُحْصِنُونَ**—we find that he (peace be upon him) did not build his discourse on the basis of informing them of what would face them of seven fertile years and then seven years of drought. Had he intended that, the proper discourse would have been to say, for example: "There will come upon you seven fertile years, then after them will come seven hard years that will take away what you have of stores," then if he were asked about repelling this famine and the way of salvation from this general destruction, he would say: "Sow for seven years consecutively..." until the end of what he said.

Rather, he built his discourse on mentioning what was obligatory upon them of action, and he made it clear that his commanding them with that was a preparation and a prelude to escaping the famine and starvation that threatened them, and this is apparent. This is evidence that what the King saw in the vision was only a representation (مثال) of what was obligatory upon him of taking measures to rescue the people from the calamity of drought, and an indication of what his duty was regarding his responsibility in the matter of his subjects. This was that he should fatten seven cows so that seven emaciated cows—which would press hard upon them—might eat them, and that he should preserve the seven green ears after they had dried in their state without threshing or winnowing.

It is as if the King's own soul witnessed in the sleep what was obligatory upon him of action against what threatened the land of the year of drought. Thus, it depicted the fertile and drought years—meaning the sustenance they would be provided with therein—in the form of the cow. Then it depicted what was in the first seven of the multiplication of the harvest by sowing consecutively in the form of fatness, and what was in the latter seven in the form of emaciation. It depicted the exhaustion of what they stored in the first seven during the second seven by the lean ones eating the fat ones. And it depicted what was obligatory upon them in preserving the stores of sustenance by the dry ears against the green ears.

Yusuf (peace be upon him) did not add anything to that in his interpretation except for three matters:

The first is what he excepted by his saying: **إِلَّا قَلِيلاً مِمَّا تَأْكُلُونَ** — *“except a little that you eat.”* This is not a part of the interpretation; rather, it is a permission and a clarification of the amount of permissible usage of what they must leave in its ear.

The second is his saying: **إِلَّا قَلِيلاً مِمَّا تُحْصِنُونَ** — *“except a little which you preserve.”* This is what they must store for the year in which the people will be helped and in which they will press, to be taken as seed and an emergency supply. It is as if he (peace be upon him) took it from his saying in the narration of the vision: **يَأْكُلُهُنَّ سَبْعٌ عِجافٌ** — *“seven lean ones were eating them,”* where he did not say "they ate them" (أكلتهن) but expressed their engagement in eating them while they had not yet finished them by eating all of them. Had their stores been exhausted in the seven hard years, he would have seen that they ate them to the very last.

The third is his saying: **ثُمَّ يَأْتِي مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ عامٌ فِيهِ يُغاثُ النَّاسُ وَفِيهِ يَعْصِرُونَ** — *“Then after that there will come a year in which the people will be helped and in which they will press.”* It is apparent that he (peace be upon him) derived this from the number seven which was repeated in the fat and lean cows and the green ears. His saying: "Then after that there will come a year," although it is an informing in form about the future, is a metaphor (كناية) for the fact that this year which will face them after the passing of the seven hard years is in no need of their diligence in the matter of sowing and storing, and no duty is directed toward them therein regarding the sustenance of the people.

Perhaps for these three reasons, he changed the style and said: **فِيهِ يُغاثُ النَّاسُ وَفِيهِ يَعْصِرُونَ** — *“in which the people will be helped and in which they will press,”* and he did not say: "in which you will be helped and in which you will press" by following the mode of address in the two previous verses. In this is an indication that the people in this year are in no need of your diligence in the matter of their livelihood and your undertaking the management of their sustenance; rather, they are helped and they press due to the descent of bounty and blessing in a fertile year.

From here, the refutation of what the author of *al-Manār* mentioned in his previous words—that this specification was not known to Yusuf (peace be upon him) except through revelation from Allah and has no counterpart in the King's vision—becomes clear.

For the replacement of the years of drought with a year of fertility is among what is derived from the vision without any doubt. As for what he mentioned regarding this year having a distinction over other fertile years in the abundance of sustenance, there is no evidence for it from the perspective of the wording at all.

From what we have mentioned, the subtle point also appears in the omission of describing the dry ears in the verse with the number seven, where it was said: **وَسَبْعَ سُنْبُلاتٍ خُضْرٍ وَأُخَرَ يابِساتٍ** — *“and seven green ears and others [that were] dry.”* For you have known that the vision does not manifest the event of fertility and drought itself, but rather manifests what the practical duty (التكليف العملي) is against the event. Thus, describing the dry ears as seven would be redundant and unnecessary, unlike if it were an indication of the years of drought themselves. Understand this.

From what has preceded, it also appears that it is more suitable for the meaning of His saying **يُغاثُ** and His saying **يَعْصِرُونَ** to be rain or the sprouting of herbage and the milking of livestock, because that is what is suitable for what he saw in his sleep of the seven cows, fat and lean, for this is what is familiar. From this, the reason for specifying "help" (الغيث) and "pressing" (العصر) for mention in this verse becomes clear. And Allah knows best.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقالَ الْمَلِكُ ائْتُونِي بِهِ فَلَمَّا جاءَهُ الرَّسُولُ قالَ ارْجِعْ إِلى رَبِّكَ فَسْئَلْهُ ما بالُ النِّسْوَةِ اللَّاتِي قَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ إِنَّ رَبِّي بِكَيْدِهِنَّ عَلِيمٌ** — *“The king said, ‘Bring him to me!’ When the messenger came to him, he said, ‘Go back to your master and ask him, ‘‘What was the case of the women who cut their hands?’’ Indeed my Lord knows their stratagems.’”* (12:50). In the discourse, there is an omission and an implied meaning for the sake of conciseness. The implied meaning—based on what the context and the consideration of the nature of circumstances indicate—is: "The messenger, who was the cupbearer, came and informed them of what Yusuf mentioned of the interpretation of the vision, and the King said after hearing it: 'Bring him to me.'"

It is clear that what he informed them of regarding the drought of seven successive years was a momentous matter, and the opinion he pointed to, which was clearly correct, was even more momentous and more wondrous to the King, who was concerned with the matter of his people and attentive to the affairs of his kingdom. What he heard had frightened and amazed him; therefore, he commanded his presence so that he might speak to him and gain further insight from what he would say. This is attested to by what Allah, the Exalted, narrated of his speaking to him in His saying: **فَلَمَّا جاءَهُ** — *“When he came to him”* and **كَلَّمَهُ** — *“he spoke to him”* etc.

His command to bring him was not to summon him [as a prisoner] but to release him from prison and summon him for conversation. Had it been a summoning and bringing of a prisoner who would return to prison after the conversation, Yusuf (peace be upon him) would not have been able to decline the presence; rather, he would have been compelled to it. Instead, it was a bringing based on pardon and release, so it was within his capacity to decline the presence and ask him to judge concerning him with truth. The result of this refusal and request was that the King said a second time: "Bring him to me, I will take him for my own," after he had said first: "Bring him to me."

He (peace be upon him) observed a magnificent etiquette in his saying to the messenger: **ارْجِعْ إِلى رَبِّكَ فَسْئَلْهُ ما بالُ النِّسْوَةِ اللَّاتِي قَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ** — *“Go back to your master and ask him, ‘What was the case of the women who cut their hands?’”* He did not mention the wife of the *‘Azīz* with what would displease him, even though he only wanted him to judge between him and her. He only pointed to the women who had solicited him, and he did not mention them with evil either, except by a matter through whose investigation his innocence would appear—and not just his innocence from the solicitation of the wife of the *‘Azīz*, but his purity from any solicitation or indecency (فحشاء) attributed to him, for his trial had been great.

He did not mention anything of dislike regarding them except what is in his saying: **إِنَّ رَبِّي بِكَيْدِهِنَّ عَلِيمٌ** — *“Indeed my Lord knows their stratagems,”* which is nothing but a type of venting his complaint to his Lord.

How subtle is his saying at the beginning of the verse and its end, where he says to the messenger: **ارْجِعْ إِلى رَبِّكَ فَسْئَلْهُ** — *“Go back to your master and ask him,”* then he says: **إِنَّ رَبِّي بِكَيْدِهِنَّ عَلِيمٌ** — *“Indeed my Lord knows their stratagems.”* In this is a type of conveying the truth, and let there be in it a reminder for whoever claims that his meaning of "my lord" (ربي) in what he said to the wife of the *‘Azīz*: **إِنَّهُ رَبِّي أَحْسَنَ مَثْوايَ** — *“He is my lord, who has given me a goodly abode”* (12:23) was her husband, and that he was calling him a lord for himself.

How subtle is his saying: **ما بالُ النِّسْوَةِ اللَّاتِي قَطَّعْنَ أَيْدِيَهُنَّ** — *“What was the case of the women who cut their hands?”* The **بال** (case/state) is the matter that is of concern. He says: "What is the momentous matter and the grave affair that caused them to fall into what they fell into?" It was nothing but their passion for him and their infatuation in his love until they forgot themselves and cut their hands instead of the fruit. Let the King reflect within himself that the trial with such infatuated lovers is very great, and refraining from their love and refusing to answer what they desire—while they would sacrifice themselves and their wealth for him—is even greater. The solicitation was not once or twice, nor was the insistence and persistence for a day or two; resistance and steadfastness against that would not be possible except for one from whom Allah turned away evil and indecency by a proof from Him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ ما خَطْبُكُنَّ إِذْ راوَدْتُنَّ يُوسُفَ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ قُلْنَ حاشَ لِلَّهِ ما عَلِمْنا عَلَيْهِ مِنْ سُوءٍ** — *“He said, ‘What was your business, women, when you solicited Yusuf?’ They said, ‘God forbid! We know of no evil in him.’”* (12:51). Al-Raghib said: **الخطب** (business/affair) is the momentous matter about which there is much discourse (تخاطب). The Exalted said: **فَما خَطْبُكَ يا سامِرِيُّ** — *“What is your business, O Samiri?”* (20:95) and **فَما خَطْبُكُمْ أَيُّهَا الْمُرْسَلُونَ** — *“What is your business, O messengers?”* (15:57). End quote.

He also said: **حصحص الحق** — *“the truth has become manifest”*—meaning it has become clear, and that is by the uncovering of what it manifests. *Hassa* and *hasshassa* are like *kaffa* and *kaffkaffa*, and *kabba* and *kabkabba*. *Hissah* (حصة) is a piece cut from it, either directly or by judgment—until he said—and *al-hissah* is the portion from the whole, and it is used in the sense of a share (النصيب). End quote.

His saying: **قالَ ما خَطْبُكُنَّ إِذْ راوَدْتُنَّ يُوسُفَ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ؟** — *“He said, ‘What was your business, women, when you solicited Yusuf?’”* is an answer to an implied question based on the omission and implied meaning in the discourse—all of which is indicated by the context. The implied meaning is: "A questioner was asking, saying: 'What happened after that? And what did the King do?' It was said: 'The messenger returned to the King and informed him of what Yusuf said and his request for judgment. So he summoned the women and asked them about the momentous matter of their affair in their solicitation of Yusuf: What was your business when you solicited Yusuf? They said: God forbid! We know of no evil in him.'" Thus, they declared him pure of every evil and testified that nothing that would displease appeared to them from him when they solicited him.

Their mention of the word of glorification: **حاشَ لِلَّهِ** — *“God forbid!”*—similar to their glorification when they saw him for the first time: **حاشَ لِلَّهِ ما هذا بَشَراً** — *“God forbid! This is no mortal!”* (12:31)—indicates his (peace be upon him) reaching the ultimate end in purity and chastity in what they knew, just as he was ultimate in beauty.

The discourse in the section of His saying: **قالَتِ امْرَأَةُ الْعَزِيزِ** — *“The wife of the ‘Aziz said”* is similar to the discourse in His saying "He said, 'What was your business...'" and His saying "They said, 'God forbid!'" At that point, the wife of the *‘Azīz* spoke—and she was the root of this trial—and confessed her sin and confirmed Yusuf (peace be upon him) in what he was claiming of innocence. She said: "Now the truth has become manifest and clear, and it is: I solicited him, and he is indeed among the truthful." Thus, she attributed the solicitation to herself and called herself a liar in accusing him of solicitation. She was not content with that but exonerated him completely, stating that he did not solicit nor did he answer her in her solicitation with obedience.

Thereby, his (peace be upon him) innocence became clear from every aspect. In the words of the women and the words of the wife of the *‘Azīz*, there are aspects of extreme emphasis in that, such as the negation of "evil" (سوء) from him using the indefinite noun in the context of negation with the addition of *min* (من): **ما عَلِمْنا عَلَيْهِ مِنْ سُوءٍ** — *“We know of no evil in him,”* along with the word of glorification: **حاشَ لِلَّهِ** — *“God forbid!”* in their saying; and her confession of the sin in the mode of restriction (حصر): **أَنَا راوَدْتُهُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ** — *“It was I who solicited him,”* and her testimony to his truthfulness emphasized by *inna* (إن), the *lām* (اللام), and the nominal sentence: **وَإِنَّهُ لَمِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ** — *“and he is indeed among the truthful,”* and other things in her speech. This negates from him (peace be upon him) every evil, more general than indecency and solicitation of her, and any inclination or tendency toward it, and lying and fabrication, through a purity arising from his good choice.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذلِكَ لِيَعْلَمَ أَنِّي لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِالْغَيْبِ وَأَنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي كَيْدَ الْخائِنِينَ** — *“[Yusuf said,] ‘That was so that he may know that I did not betray him in [his] absence, and that Allah does not guide the stratagems of the treacherous.’”* (12:52). This is from the speech of Yusuf (peace be upon him) as indicated by the context. It is as if he said it upon the testimony of the women to the innocence of his side from every evil, the confession of the wife of the *‘Azīz* to the sin, her testimony to his truthfulness, and the King's judgment of his innocence.

The narration of speech [without an explicit "he said"] has many counterparts in the Quran, such as His saying: **آمَنَ الرَّسُولُ بِما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ رَبِّهِ وَالْمُؤْمِنُونَ كُلٌّ آمَنَ بِاللهِ وَمَلائِكَتِهِ وَكُتُبِهِ وَرُسُلِهِ لا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِنْ رُسُلِهِ** — *“The Apostle has faith in what has been sent down to him from his Lord, and all the faithful [as well]. Each one has faith in Allah, His angels, His Books and His apostles. [Saying,] ‘We make no distinction between any of His apostles.’”* (2:285)—meaning they said: "We make no distinction..."—and His saying: **وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الصَّافُّونَ وَإِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الْمُسَبِّحُونَ** — *“Indeed we are those who stand in ranks, and indeed we are those who celebrate His glory.”* (37:165-166).

Based on this, the indication in His saying **ذلِكَ** — *“That”* is to the returning of the messenger to the King and his request for judgment. The pronoun in **لِيَعْلَمَ** — *“so that he may know”* and **لَمْ أَخُنْهُ** — *“I did not betray him”* refers to the *‘Azīz*. The meaning is: "I only returned the messenger to the King and asked him to investigate the matter and judge with truth so that the *‘Azīz* may know that I did not betray him in his absence by soliciting his wife, and so that he may know that Allah does not guide the stratagems of the treacherous."

He (peace be upon him) mentions two goals for what he did of returning [the messenger] and the request:

**One of them:** That the *‘Azīz* may know that he did not betray him, so that his soul may be at peace regarding him and any doubt or suspicion regarding him and his affair may vanish.

**The second:** That he may know that the treacherous one, absolutely, does not attain his goal through his treachery, and that he will inevitably be exposed—the custom of Allah (سنة الله) which has passed among His servants, and you will never find any change in the custom of Allah. For treachery is from falsehood (الباطل), and falsehood does not endure and the truth will surely manifest over it. Had the treacherous one been guided to his desire, the women who cut their hands and took to solicitation would not have been exposed, nor would the wife of the *‘Azīz* in what she did and persisted in; for Allah does not guide the stratagems of the treacherous.

The purpose of the second goal—**وَأَنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي كَيْدَ الْخائِنِينَ** — *“and that Allah does not guide the stratagems of the treacherous”*—and his reminding and teaching it to the King, was to obtain the necessary implication of the benefit of the report (لازم فائدة الخبر), which is that the King may know that he (peace be upon him) is knowledgeable of that and submissive to its reality. Thus, if he did not betray him in his honor in his absence, and does not betray in anything at all, he would be worthy of being entrusted with everything, whether it be life, honor, or wealth.

By this clear distinction, there was prepared for Yusuf what was in his mind to ask of the King, which was his subsequent saying after he was brought before the King: **اجْعَلْنِي عَلى خَزائِنِ الْأَرْضِ إِنِّي حَفِيظٌ عَلِيمٌ** — *“‘Set me over the treasuries of the land. Indeed I am a knowing keeper.’”* (12:55).

The verse is apparent in that this King is other than the *‘Azīz* of Egypt, the husband of the woman, who was pointed to by His saying: **وَأَلْفَيا سَيِّدَها لَدَى الْبابِ** — *“they found her husband at the door”* (12:25) and His saying: **وَقالَ الَّذِي اشْتَراهُ مِنْ مِصْرَ لِامْرَأَتِهِ أَكْرِمِي مَثْواهُ** — *“The man from Egypt who had bought him said to his wife, ‘Entertain him honourably...’”* (12:21).

Some commentators have mentioned that this verse and the one after it are a continuation of the speech of the wife of the *‘Azīz*: **الْآنَ حَصْحَصَ الْحَقُّ أَنَا راوَدْتُهُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ وَإِنَّهُ لَمِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ** — *“Now the truth has become manifest. It was I who solicited him, and he is indeed among the truthful.”* (12:51). The discussion on this will follow.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما أُبَرِّئُ نَفْسِي إِنَّ النَّفْسَ لَأَمَّارَةٌ بِالسُّوءِ إِلَّا ما رَحِمَ رَبِّي إِنَّ رَبِّي غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — *“‘Yet I do not acquit my own soul. Indeed the soul is prone to evil, except those on whom my Lord has mercy. Indeed my Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful.’”* (12:53). This is the continuation of the speech of Yusuf (peace be upon him). This is because his saying: **أَنِي لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِالْغَيْبِ** — *“that I did not betray him in [his] absence”* was not free from a taint of claiming power and strength (الحول والقوة). He (peace be upon him) is among the sincere ones (المخلصين) who are deeply rooted in monotheism (التوحيد), who do not see power or strength for any other than Him, the Exalted. Thus, he (peace be upon him) hastened to negate power and strength from himself and to attribute what appeared from him of righteous action or beautiful attribute to the mercy of his Lord, and to equate his soul with all other souls which, by nature, are inclined toward passions and prone to evil. He said: **وَما أُبَرِّئُ نَفْسِي إِنَّ النَّفْسَ لَأَمَّارَةٌ بِالسُّوءِ إِلَّا ما رَحِمَ رَبِّي** — *“Yet I do not acquit my own soul. Indeed the soul is prone to evil, except those on whom my Lord has mercy.”* This saying of his is like the saying of Shu‘ayb (peace be upon him): **إِنْ أُرِيدُ إِلَّا الْإِصْلاحَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُ وَما تَوْفِيقِي إِلَّا بِاللهِ** — *“I only desire reform, so far as I am able. My success lies only with Allah...”* (11:88).

So his saying: **وَما أُبَرِّئُ نَفْسِي** — *“Yet I do not acquit my own soul”* is an indication of his saying: **أَنِي لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِالْغَيْبِ** — *“that I did not betray him in [his] absence,”* and that he did not say this speech with the motive of exonerating his soul and purifying it, but rather with the motive of narrating a mercy from his Lord. He justified that by his saying: **إِنَّ النَّفْسَ لَأَمَّارَةٌ بِالسُّوءِ** — *“Indeed the soul is prone to evil,”* meaning that the soul by its nature calls toward its desires of evils in their abundance and plenty; thus, it is from ignorance to be acquitted of the inclination toward evil. One only refrains from its command to evil and its call to wickedness by a mercy from Allah, the Glorified, which turns it away from evil and grants it success for righteous action.

From here it appears that His saying: **إِلَّا ما رَحِمَ رَبِّي** — *“except those on whom my Lord has mercy”* yields two benefits:

**One of them:** Restricting the generality of his saying: **إِنَّ النَّفْسَ لَأَمَّارَةٌ بِالسُّوءِ** — *“Indeed the soul is prone to evil,”* thus indicating that the performance of good deeds—which is by the mercy of Allah, the Glorified—is from the command of the soul and does not occur out of compulsion or coercion from His side, the Exalted.

**The second of them:** An indication that his avoiding treachery was by the mercy of his Lord.

He justified the ruling by his saying: **إِنَّ رَبِّي غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — *“Indeed my Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful,”* so he added His forgiveness, the Exalted, to His mercy. This is because through forgiveness, the deficiency (النقيصة) inherent in nature is veiled, and through mercy, the beautiful matter is manifested. His forgiveness, the Exalted, just as it erases sins and their effects, likewise veils deficiencies and their consequences, and it pertains to all deficiencies just as it pertains to sins. He, the Exalted, said: **فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ غَيْرَ باغٍ وَلا عادٍ فَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — *“But should someone be compelled, without being rebellious or transitory, indeed your Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful.”* (6:145). The discussion on this has preceded at the end of the sixth volume of the book.

Among the subtleties in his speech of indication is his (peace be upon him) expression for Allah, exalted is His Name, with the term **رَبِّي** (my Lord). He repeated it three times, saying: **إِنَّ رَبِّي بِكَيْدِهِنَّ عَلِيمٌ** — *“Indeed my Lord knows their stratagems,”* **إِلَّا ما رَحِمَ رَبِّي** — *“except those on whom my Lord has mercy,”* and **إِنَّ رَبِّي غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — *“Indeed my Lord is all-forgiving, all-merciful.”* This is because these sentences encompass a type of favor from his Lord toward him, so he praised the Lord, the Exalted, by attributing Him to himself to convey his creed, which is monotheism (التوحيد) by taking Allah, the Glorified, as a Lord for himself and an object of worship, contrary to the polytheists. As for his saying: **وَأَنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي كَيْدَ الْخائِنِينَ** — *“and that Allah does not guide the stratagems of the treacherous,”* it is free from this attribution, and therefore he expressed it with the Majestic Name (لفظ الجلالة).

A group of commentators have mentioned that the two verses—namely His saying: **ذلِكَ لِيَعْلَمَ أَنِّي لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِالْغَيْبِ** — *“That was so that he may know that I did not betray him in [his] absence”* etc.—are from the completion of the speech of the wife of the *‘Azīz*. The meaning according to this is that the wife of the *‘Azīz*, when she confessed her sin and testified to his truthfulness, said: **ذلِكَ** — *“That”*—meaning my confession that I solicited him and my testimony that he is among the truthful—**لِيَعْلَمَ** — *“is so that he may know”*—when this speech of mine reaches him—**أَنِّي لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِالْغَيْبِ** — *“that I did not betray him in [his] absence,”* but rather I confessed that the solicitation was from my side and that he was truthful; **وَأَنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي كَيْدَ الْخائِنِينَ** — *“and that Allah does not guide the stratagems of the treacherous,”* just as He did not guide my own stratagem when I plotted against him with types of solicitation and with prison for several years, until He manifested his truthfulness in his speech, the purity of his hem, and the innocence of his soul, and exposed me before the King and the assembly; and He did not guide the stratagem of the other women in their solicitation; **وَما أُبَرِّئُ نَفْسِي** — *“Yet I do not acquit my own soul”* from evil absolutely, for I plotted against him with prison so that he might be forced by it to do what I command him; **إِنَّ النَّفْسَ لَأَمَّارَةٌ بِالسُّوءِ إِلَّا ما رَحِمَ رَبِّي إِنَّ رَبِّي غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ**.

This is a very poor view. Firstly: because His saying: **ذلِكَ لِيَعْلَمَ أَنِّي لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِالْغَيْبِ** — *“That was so that he may know that I did not betray him in [his] absence,”* if it were from the speech of the wife of the *‘Azīz*, the proper discourse would have been to say: "and so that he may know that I [now] betray him not in his absence"—in the imperative or present form. For the word **ذلِكَ** — *“That”* in this view is an indication of her confession of the sin and her testimony to his truthfulness. Thus, His saying: **لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِالْغَيْبِ** — *“I did not betray him in [his] absence,”* if it were a title for her confession and testimony pointed to by it, the discourse would be devoid of benefit. For the result of its meaning then would be: "I only confessed and testified so that he may know that I confessed and testified for him in his absence." Additionally, that would invalidate the meaning of confession and testimony, for it would indicate that she only confessed and testified so that Yusuf might hear that and know of it, not for the manifestation of the truth and the clarification of the reality of the matter.

If it were a title for her actions during the long period of his absence while he remained several years in prison—meaning "I only confessed and testified for him so that he may know that I did not betray him during the length of his absence"—then she *did* betray him when she plotted against him so that he was imprisoned and remained in prison for several years. Furthermore, her confession and testimony do not indicate her lack of betrayal of him in any way, as is apparent.

Secondly: because there would be no meaning then for her teaching Yusuf that Allah does not guide the stratagems of the treacherous, while Yusuf had reminded her of it at the very first when she solicited him, saying: **إِنَّهُ لا يُفْلِحُ الظَّالِمُونَ** — *“Indeed the wrongdoers will not be successful.”* (12:23).

Thirdly: because her saying: "Yet I do not acquit my own soul, for I betrayed him by plotting against him with prison" would contradict her saying: "I did not betray him in [his] absence," as is not hidden. Additionally, His saying: **إِنَّ النَّفْسَ لَأَمَّارَةٌ بِالسُّوءِ إِلَّا ما رَحِمَ رَبِّي إِنَّ رَبِّي غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ**, with what it contains of sublime monotheistic gnosis (المعارف الجليلة التوحيدية), is not likely to issue from a woman surrounded by passions who worships idols.

Some have mentioned another view in the meaning of the two verses by referring the pronoun in **لِيَعْلَمَ** — *“so that he may know”* and **لَمْ أَخُنْهُ** — *“I did not betray him”* to the *‘Azīz*, who is her husband. So she is as if saying: "That which occurred, I have confessed to it so that my husband may know that I did not betray him in deed in what occurred of my private moments with Yusuf in his absence from us, and that all that happened was that I solicited him and he sought refuge and refused, so the honor of my husband remained protected and his dignity preserved. And if I have acquitted Yusuf of the sin, I do not acquit my own soul of it, for the soul is prone to evil except those on whom my Lord has mercy."

In this: the discourse, if it were from her speech and she intended by it to put her husband's soul at peace and remove any doubt from his heart, would produce the opposite of what is desired. For her saying: **الْآنَ حَصْحَصَ الْحَقُّ أَنَا راوَدْتُهُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ وَإِنَّهُ لَمِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ** — *“Now the truth has become manifest. It was I who solicited him, and he is indeed among the truthful”* only yields the knowledge that she solicited him. As for her testimony that he refused and did not obey her in what she commanded him, it is a testimony for herself, not against her. It was possible that she only testified for him to put her husband's soul at peace and remove what he had of doubt and suspicion. Thus, her confession and testimony do not necessitate in themselves the *‘Azīz's* knowledge that she did not betray him in his absence.

Additionally, His saying: **وَما أُبَرِّئُ نَفْسِي** — *“Yet I do not acquit my own soul”* etc., would then be a repetition of the meaning of her saying: **أَنَا راوَدْتُهُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ** — *“It was I who solicited him,”* and the apparent context is contrary to that. Furthermore, some of the objections raised against the previous view are applicable to this one.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقالَ الْمَلِكُ ائْتُونِي بِهِ أَسْتَخْلِصْهُ لِنَفْسِي فَلَمَّا كَلَّمَهُ قالَ إِنَّكَ الْيَوْمَ لَدَيْنا مَكِينٌ أَمِينٌ** — *“The king said, ‘Bring him to me, I will take him for my own.’ When he had spoken with him, he said, ‘Today you are with us a person of standing and trust.’”* (12:54). It is said: *astakhlisahu* (I take him for my own), meaning he made him pure (خالصاً). **المكين** (person of standing) is the possessor of place (مكانة) and rank. In His saying: **فَلَمَّا كَلَّمَهُ** — *“When he had spoken with him,”* there is an omission for conciseness, and the implied meaning is: "When he was brought to him and he spoke with him, he said: 'Indeed you are today...'" etc. In restricting the ruling to "today" (اليوم), there is an indication of the justification (التعليل). The meaning is: "Indeed you are today—now that there has appeared of your noble character in avoiding evil, indecency, treachery, and injustice, and your patience upon every dislike and humiliation in the way of the purity of your soul, and your being singled out for an unseen support from your Lord and knowledge of the interpretation of events, and your opinion, prudence, wisdom, and intellect—with us a possessor of rank and trust." He made His saying **مَكِينٌ أَمِينٌ** — *“person of standing and trust”* absolute, thus yielding the generality of the ruling.

The meaning is: The King said, "Bring Yusuf to me, I will make him pure for myself and a special one for me." When he was brought to him and he spoke with him, he said to him: "Indeed you are today—now that there has appeared of your perfection what has appeared—with us a possessor of absolute rank and absolute trust; you are empowered over all that you desire and entrusted with all the affairs of the kingdom." In that was the ruling of his premiership (صدارته).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ اجْعَلْنِي عَلى خَزائِنِ الْأَرْضِ إِنِّي حَفِيظٌ عَلِيمٌ** — *“He said, ‘Set me over the treasuries of the land. Indeed I am a knowing keeper.’”* (12:55). When the King pledged to Yusuf that "Today you are with us a person of standing and trust" and made the statement absolute, Yusuf (peace be upon him) asked him to appoint him over the treasuries of the land and delegate their matter to him. What is meant by "the land" (الأرض) is the land of Egypt.

He only asked what he asked so that he might personally undertake the management of the matter of provisions (الميرة) and the sustenance of the people, so that he might gather it and store it for the seven hard years that would face the people and bring upon them their drought and famine; and so that he might personally stand for the distribution of sustenance among the people and giving each of them what he deserves of provisions without any injustice (حيف).

He justified his request for that by his saying: **إِنِّي حَفِيظٌ عَلِيمٌ** — *“Indeed I am a knowing keeper.”* For these two attributes are those whose presence is necessary in one who undertakes a position that he is asking for, and he cannot do without them. He was answered in what he asked and engaged in what he desired; all of that is known from the context of the verses and what follows them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكَذلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ يَتَبَوَّأُ مِنْها حَيْثُ يَشاءُ نُصِيبُ بِرَحْمَتِنا مَنْ نَشاءُ وَلا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — *“Thus did We establish Yusuf in the land, to settle in it wherever he wished. We confer Our mercy on whomever We wish, and We do not waste the reward of the virtuous.”* (12:56). **التمكين** (establishment) is the granting of power (الإقدار), and **التبوء** (settling) is the taking of a place.

The indication in His saying **كَذلِكَ** — *“Thus”* is to what He narrated of the story in how it ended with his (peace be upon him) attaining the might of Egypt. It is the account of the prison; the wife of the *‘Azīz* had threatened him with humiliation through prison, so Allah made it a cause for might. In this manner his (peace be upon him) affair proceeded: his father honored him, so his brothers envied him and plotted against him by casting him into the bottom of the pit and selling him to the caravan to humiliate him, so Allah honored his abode in the house of the *‘Azīz*. The wife of the *‘Azīz* and the women of Egypt plotted against him to bring him to the place of wickedness, so Allah manifested his protection (عصمته). Then she plotted against him with prison for his humiliation, so Allah caused that to be the reason for his might.

To point to the matter of the prison, his incarceration, and the stripping of his freedom of association and social life, He, the Exalted, said: **وَكَذلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ يَتَبَوَّأُ مِنْها حَيْثُ يَشاءُ** — *“Thus did We establish Yusuf in the land, to settle in it wherever he wished.”* Meaning: We lifted from him the distress of the prison which had stripped from him the freedom of will, so he became of absolute volition, having the right to settle in any spot he wished. This speech, in one way, parallels His saying, the Exalted, previously regarding him when he entered the house of the *‘Azīz* and his wife was advised: **وَكَذلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَلِنُعَلِّمَهُ مِنْ تَأْوِيلِ الْأحادِيثِ وَاللهُ غالِبٌ عَلى أَمْرِهِ** — *“Thus did We establish Yusuf in the land, that We might teach him the interpretation of dreams. Allah has full command of His affairs...”* (12:21).

By this comparison, it appears that His saying here: **نُصِيبُ بِرَحْمَتِنا مَنْ نَشاءُ** — *“We confer Our mercy on whomever We wish”* is in the meaning of His saying there: **وَاللهُ غالِبٌ عَلى أَمْرِهِ** — *“Allah has full command of His affairs.”* And the meaning is that Allah, the Glorified, when He wishes to confer His mercy on someone, He is not overcome in His wish, and it is not within the capacity of any assumed preventer to prevent His conferring it. Had it been within the capacity of a cause to invalidate the wish of Allah regarding someone, it would have been possible in Yusuf, against whom the decisive causes collaborated and manifested to lower him, yet Allah raised him; and to humiliate him, yet Allah honored him. Indeed, judgment belongs only to Allah.

His saying: **وَلا نُضِيعُ أَجْرَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — *“and We do not waste the reward of the virtuous”* is an indication that this establishment is a reward given to Yusuf (peace be upon him), and a beautiful promise to all the virtuous (المحسنين) that Allah does not waste their reward.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَأَجْرُ الْآخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكانُوا يَتَّقُونَ** — *“And the reward of the Hereafter is surely better for those who have faith and are God-conscious.”* (12:57). Meaning for the friends of Allah (أولياء الله) among His servants. So it is a beautiful otherworldly promise specifically for His friends, the Exalted, and Yusuf (peace be upon him) was among them.

The evidence that this does not encompass the generality of the believers (المؤمنين) is the circumstantial clause (الجملة الحالية): **وَكانُوا يَتَّقُونَ** (and they used to be God-conscious), which indicates that this faith (الإيمان)—which is the reality of faith (حقيقة الإيمان)—was inevitably preceded by a continuous and true godwariness (تقوى). This godwariness (التقوى) — *the inner state of being cautious of Allah’s boundaries and preserving the soul from what displeases Him* — cannot be realized without faith; thus, it is faith after faith and godwariness, which is synonymous with the friendship of Allah (ولاية الله), the Glorified. The Exalted says: **أَلا إِنَّ أَوْلِياءَ اللهِ لا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكانُوا يَتَّقُونَ لَهُمُ الْبُشْرى فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَفِي الْآخِرَةِ** "Look! The friends of Allah will indeed have no fear nor will they grieve (62) those who have faith and are God-conscious. (63) For them is good news in the life of this world and in the Hereafter." (Yunus: 62-64).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: "Then the King saw a vision, and he said to his ministers: **إني رأيت في نومي سبع بقرات سمان ـ يأكلهن سبع عجاف** (I saw in my sleep seven fat cows being eaten by seven lean ones)—meaning emaciated—**ورأيت سبع سنبلات خضر وأخر يابسات** (and I saw seven green ears of grain and others that were dry)." Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام said: "Seven ears (سنابل)." Then he [the King] said: **يا أيها الملأ أفتوني في رؤياي إن كنتم للرؤيا تعبرون** "O council! Give me your opinion regarding my dream, if you can interpret dreams." But they did not know its interpretation.

Then the one who used to be at the King's head remembered the vision he had seen, and he remembered Yusuf after seven years; this is His saying: **وَقالَ الَّذِي نَجا مِنْهُما وَادَّكَرَ بَعْدَ أُمَّةٍ** "The one of the two who had been delivered said, remembering after a long time," meaning after a period. **أَنَا أُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِتَأْوِيلِهِ فَأَرْسِلُونِ** "I will inform you of its interpretation, so let me go." He came to Yusuf and said: **أَيُّهَا الصِّدِّيقُ أَفْتِنا فِي سَبْعِ بَقَراتٍ سِمانٍ ـ يَأْكُلُهُنَّ سَبْعٌ عِجافٌ ـ وَسَبْعِ سُنْبُلاتٍ خُضْرٍ وَأُخَرَ يابِساتٍ** "O truthful one! Give us your opinion regarding seven fat cows eaten by seven lean ones, and seven green ears and [seven] others that were dry."

Yusuf said: **تزرعون سبع سنين دأبا ـ فما حصدتم فذروه في سنبله إلا قليلا مما تأكلون** "You will sow for seven years consecutively. Then leave what you harvest in its ear, except a little that you eat"—meaning do not thresh it, for it would spoil over the course of seven years, but while it is in its ear, it does not spoil—**ثم يأتي من بعد ذلك سبع شداد ـ يأكلن ما قدمتم لهن** "Then after that there will come seven hard years which will consume what you have set aside for them" during the past seven years. Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام said: "It was revealed as: **ما قربتم لهن** (what you brought near for them)." **ثم يأتي من بعد ذلك عام فيه يغاث الناس ـ وفيه يعصرون** "Then after that there will come a year in which the people will be helped and in which they will press [grapes]"—meaning it will rain upon them.

Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام said: "A man recited before the Commander of the Faithful عليه‌السلام: **ثُمَّ يَأْتِي مِنْ بَعْدِ ذلِكَ عامٌ فِيهِ يُغاثُ النَّاسُ وَفِيهِ يَعْصِرُونَ** (Then after that there will come a year in which the people will be helped and in which they will press)—using the active voice (البناء للفاعل). He [the Imam] said: 'Woe to you! What will they press? Will they press wine?' The man asked: 'O Commander of the Faithful, how should I recite it?' He replied: 'It was revealed as: **وفيه يعصرون** (and in it they shall be rained upon)'—meaning they will receive rain after the years of famine. The evidence for this is His saying: **وَأَنْزَلْنا مِنَ الْمُعْصِراتِ ماءً ثَجَّاجاً** 'And sent down from the rain-clouds (المعصرات) abundant water.'"

The man returned to the King and informed him of what Yusuf had said. The King said: **ائتوني به** "Bring him to me." When the messenger came to him, Yusuf said: **ارجع إلى ربك** "Go back to your lord"—meaning the King—**فاسأله ما بال النسوة اللاتي قطعن أيديهن؟ إن ربي بكيدهن عليم** "and ask him, 'What was the case of the women who cut their hands?' Indeed my Lord knows their stratagems."

The King gathered the women and said: **ما خطبكن ـ إذ راودتن يوسف عن نفسه؟** "What was your business, when you sought to seduce Yusuf?" They said: **حاش لله ما علمنا عليه من سوء** "God forbid! We know of no evil in him." The wife of the *Aziz* said: **الآنَ حَصْحَصَ الْحَقُّ أَنَا راوَدْتُهُ عَنْ نَفْسِهِ ـ وَإِنَّهُ لَمِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ** "Now the truth has come to light! It was I who sought to seduce him, and he is indeed one of the truthful. (51) **ذلك ليعلم أني لم أخنه بالغيب ـ وأن الله لا يهدي كيد الخائنين** [I say] this, so that he may know that I did not betray him in [his] absence, and that Allah does not guide the stratagems of the treacherous"—meaning I do not lie against him now as I lied against him before. Then she said: **وَما أُبَرِّئُ نَفْسِي إِنَّ النَّفْسَ لأَمَّارَةٌ بِالسُّوءِ ـ إِلَّا ما رَحِمَ رَبِّي** "Yet I do not acquit my own soul. Indeed the soul (النفس) — *the lower self or carnal soul* — is an instigator of evil, except such as my Lord has mercy on."

The King said: **ائْتُونِي بِهِ أَسْتَخْلِصْهُ لِنَفْسِي** "Bring him to me, I will take him for my own service." When he looked at Yusuf, he said: **إِنَّكَ الْيَوْمَ لَدَيْنا مَكِينٌ أَمِينٌ** "Today you are with us established and trusted." Yusuf said: "Ask for your need." He said: **اجْعَلْنِي عَلى خَزائِنِ الْأَرْضِ ـ إِنِّي حَفِيظٌ عَلِيمٌ** "Put me in charge of the country’s granaries. I am indeed a knowing keeper"—meaning the silos and storehouses. So he put him in charge of them, which is His saying: **وَكَذلِكَ مَكَّنَّا لِيُوسُفَ فِي الْأَرْضِ ـ يَتَبَوَّأُ مِنْها حَيْثُ يَشاءُ** "Thus did We establish Yusuf in the land, to settle in it wherever he wished."

**I say:** His saying: "And al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام recited: **سبع سنابل** (seven ears)"—in the narration of al-Ayyashi from Ibn Abi Ya'fur from him عليه‌السلام, he recited: **سَبْعَ سُنْبُلاتٍ** (seven ears). His saying عليه‌السلام: "It was revealed as: **ما قربتم لهن** (what you brought near for them)" means that the "setting aside" (التقديم) according to the revelation (التنزيل) carries the meaning of "bringing near" (التقريب). His saying عليه‌السلام: "It was revealed as: **وَفِيهِ يَعْصِرُونَ** (and in it they shall be rained upon)"—meaning using the passive voice (البناء للمفعول). From this, it is known that he عليه‌السلام takes the word **يغاث** (helped) from **الغيث** (rain) rather than **الغوث** (succor). Al-Ayyashi also narrated this meaning in his *Tafsir* from Ali ibn Ma'mar from his father from Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام.

The phrase: "meaning I do not lie against him now as I lied against him before" is apparent in taking the saying: **ذلِكَ لِيَعْلَمَ أَنِّي لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِالْغَيْبِ** (This, so that he may know that I did not betray him in his absence) until the end of the two verses as the speech of the wife of the *Aziz*. I have already discussed this in the preceding commentary (البيان).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Faryabi, Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated through various chains from Ibn Abbas that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "I marveled at the patience of my brother Yusuf and his nobility—and may Allah forgive him when he was sent to for the interpretation of the vision—had it been me, I would not have done so until I was released. And I marveled at his patience and nobility—may Allah forgive him—when he was invited to come out but did not come out until he informed them of his excuse; had it been me, I would have rushed to the door. But he preferred to have his excuse established."

**I say:** This meaning has been narrated through other chains. Among the chains of the People of the House (أهل البيت) عليه‌السلام is what appears in the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi from Aban from Muhammad ibn Muslim from one of the two [Imams] عليهما‌السلام, who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Had I been in the position of Yusuf when the King sent to him asking about his vision, I would not have informed him until I stipulated that he release me from prison. And I marveled at his patience regarding the matter of the King's wife until Allah made his excuse manifest.'"

**I say:** This prophetic narration (النبوي) is not without issues, for it contains one of two problematic implications. It either impugns the excellence of Yusuf's عليه‌السلام management (تدبير) and his method of securing his release from prison—whereas he managed it excellently. He did not desire a mere exit from prison while the wife of the *Aziz* and the women of Egypt were still intent on seducing him and forcing him to comply with their desires, for he was the one who said: **رَبِّ السِّجْنُ أَحَبُّ إِلَيَّ مِمَّا يَدْعُونَنِي إِلَيْهِ** "My Lord! The prison is dearer to me than what they invite me to." Rather, he wanted to exit in an atmosphere where his innocence was manifest, where the wife of the *Aziz* and the women would despair of him, and where he would be placed in a position of status and rank befitting him.

Therefore, while still in prison, he first informed them of the King's necessary duty following his vision—collecting and storing the general provisions—thereby leading to the King's command: **ائْتُونِي بِهِ** (Bring him to me). Then, when ordered to be released, he refused until a just judgment was passed between him and the women, which led to the King saying: **ائْتُونِي بِهِ أستخلصه لِنَفْسِي** (Bring him to me, I will take him for my own service). This is the best management (تدبير) conceivable for what he sought of honor in Egypt and the spreading of justice and benevolence in the land. This is in addition to what became manifest to the King and his council during these events regarding his great patience, his resolve in affairs, his endurance of harm for the sake of truth, his vast knowledge, and his sound judgment.

The second implication would be an aspersion against the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—and far be it from him to say that had he been in Yusuf's place, he would have been hasty and lacked patience, while simultaneously admitting that the truth was with Yusuf in his patience. Such an admission would imply that it is in his [the Prophet's] nature not to be patient where patience is required. Far be it from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to command people with something and forget himself, for he indeed remained patient and endured harm for the sake of Allah before and after the Migration (الهجرة) until Allah praised him with His saying: **وَإِنَّكَ لَعَلى خُلُقٍ عَظِيمٍ** "And indeed you possess a great character."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur* as well, al-Hakim in his *Tarikh*, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Daylami narrated from Anas that the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله recited this verse: **ذلِكَ لِيَعْلَمَ ـ أَنِّي لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِالْغَيْبِ** (This, so that he may know that I did not betray him in his absence). He said: "When Yusuf said it, Gabriel said to him: 'O Yusuf, remember your desire (همك).' He [Yusuf] then said: **وَما أُبَرِّئُ نَفْسِي** (Yet I do not acquit my own soul)."

**I say:** This meaning is narrated in several reports with similar wording. In the narration of Ibn Abbas: "When Yusuf said it, Gabriel nudged him and said: 'Not even when you desired her?'" In a narration from Hakim ibn Jabir: "Gabriel said to him: 'Not even when you loosened your trousers?'" Similar reports are found from Mujahid, Qatadah, Ikrimah, al-Dahhak, Ibn Zayd, al-Suddi, al-Hasan, Ibn Jurayh, Abu Salih, and others.

It has already been stated in the preceding commentary (البيان) that these and similar reports are among the fabricated narrations (موضوعات الأخبار) that contradict the explicit text of the Book. Far be it from the station of Yusuf the Truthful (الصديق) عليه‌السلام to lie by saying: **لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِالْغَيْبِ** (I did not betray him in his absence) and then attempt to fix what he spoiled due to a nudge from Gabriel. The author of *al-Kashshaf* said: "The proponents of falsehood have patched together fabricated narrations, claiming that when Yusuf said: **أَنِّي لَمْ أَخُنْهُ بِالْغَيْبِ**, Gabriel said to him: 'Not even when you desired her?' and the wife of the *Aziz* said to him: 'Not even when you loosened the drawstring of your trousers, O Yusuf?' This is due to their obsession with slandering Allah and His Messenger." End quote.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Sama'ah: "I asked him about the saying of Allah: **ارْجِعْ إِلى رَبِّكَ** (Go back to your lord)—the verse—meaning the *Aziz*."

**I say:** In *Tafsir al-Burhan*, from al-Tabarsi in the *Book of Prophethood* (كتاب النبوة), with a chain of transmission to Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Isa from al-Hasan ibn Ali ibn Ilyas, who said: "I heard al-Rida عليه‌السلام saying: 'Yusuf turned to collecting food during the seven years of plenty, storing it in the granaries. When those years passed and the years of drought arrived, Yusuf turned to selling the food. In the first year, he sold it for dirhams and dinars until there remained not a single dinar or dirham in Egypt or its surroundings except that it came into Yusuf's possession.

In the second year, he sold it for jewelry and gems until there remained no jewelry or gems in Egypt or its surroundings except that they came into his possession. In the third year, he sold it for beasts and livestock until there remained no beast or livestock in Egypt or its surroundings except that they came into his possession. In the fourth year, he sold it for male and female slaves until there remained no male or female slave in Egypt or its surroundings except that they came into his possession.
