In the fifth year, he sold it for houses and courtyards until there remained no house or courtyard in Egypt or its surroundings except that they came into his possession. In the sixth year, he sold it for farms and rivers until there remained no river or farm in Egypt or its surroundings except that they came into his possession. In the seventh year, he sold it for their own persons until there remained no slave or free person in Egypt or its surroundings except that they became slaves to Yusuf.

Thus, he owned their free and their enslaved, and their wealth. The people said: "We have never seen nor heard of a king whom Allah granted such sovereignty, wisdom, knowledge, and management." Then Yusuf said to the King: "What is your view regarding what my Lord has bestowed upon me of the sovereignty of Egypt and its surroundings? Advise us with your opinion, for I did not reform them to ruin them, nor did I save them from affliction for it to become an affliction upon them; rather, Allah saved them through my hand." The King said: "The decision is yours."

Yusuf said: "I call Allah to witness, and I call you to witness, O King, that I have emancipated all the people of Egypt, and I have returned to them their wealth and their slaves. And I have returned to you the kingdom, your signet ring, your throne, and your crown—on the condition that you do not conduct yourself except according to my conduct, nor judge except by my judgment."

The King said to him: "That is my repentance and my pride—that I do not conduct myself except according to your conduct, nor judge except by your judgment. Had it not been for you, I would not have had authority over you, nor would I have been guided to it. You have made my authority mighty and unassailable. I bear witness that there is no god but Allah alone, without partner, and that you are His Messenger. So remain over what I have entrusted to you, for you are with us established and trusted."'"

**I say:** The narrations in this regard are numerous, most of which are not related to the purpose of interpreting the verses; therefore, we have omitted their citation.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, Sulayman said: "Sufyan said: 'I said to Abu Abdillah عليه‌السلام: Is it permissible for a man to praise himself?' He said: 'Yes, when he is compelled to do so. Have you not heard the saying of Yusuf: **اجْعَلْنِي عَلى خَزائِنِ الْأَرْضِ إِنِّي حَفِيظٌ عَلِيمٌ** (Put me in charge of the country’s granaries. I am indeed a knowing keeper) and the saying of the righteous servant: **إني لكم ناصح أمين** (I am a trustworthy advisor to you).'"

**I say:** It appears that the "righteous servant" (العبد الصالح) refers to Hud, as he says to his people: **أُبَلِّغُكُمْ رِسالاتِ رَبِّي وَأَنَا لَكُمْ ناصِحٌ أَمِينٌ** "I deliver to you the messages of my Lord, and I am a trustworthy advisor to you." (al-A'raf: 68).

In *al-Uyun*, with its chain to al-Ayyashi, who said: "Muhammad ibn Nasr narrated to us from al-Hasan ibn Musa, who said: 'Our companions narrated from al-Rida عليه‌السلام that a man said to him: "May Allah set your affairs right, how did you come to be in the position you are in with al-Ma'mun?"—as if he disapproved of that for him. Abu al-Hasan al-Rida عليه‌السلام said to him: "Who is superior, a Prophet or a Successor (الوصي)?" He replied: "Rather, a Prophet." He asked: "Who is superior, a Muslim or a polytheist?" He replied: "Rather, a Muslim."

He said: "The *Aziz* of Egypt was a polytheist while Yusuf was a Prophet, and al-Ma'mun is a Muslim while I am a Successor. Yusuf asked the *Aziz* to appoint him until he said: **استعملني على خزائن الأرض إني حفيظ عليم** (Appoint me over the granaries of the land, for I am a knowing keeper), whereas al-Ma'mun compelled me into the position I am in." He [the Imam] said regarding His saying: **حَفِيظٌ عَلِيمٌ** (a knowing keeper): "A keeper (حافظ) over what is in my hand, and knowledgeable (عالم) in every tongue."'"

**I say:** His saying: "Appoint me over the granaries of the land" is a citation of the verse by its meaning. Al-Ayyashi narrated it in his *Tafsir*, and the end of the tradition was also narrated in *al-Ma'ani* from Fadl ibn Abi Qurrah from al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام.
